news.title: Nations Championship: Scotland prepared for try-fest with Fiji

news.source: ABC News & Headlines – Australian Broadcasting Corporation

sections.heading: Vakatawa returns to international stage
sections.body: Center Virimi Vakatawa will make his Fiji debut against Scotland as the Flying Fijians look to recover from recent defeats. Vakatawa returns to international rugby after previously earning 32 caps for France. While the match is officially a Fiji home game, the squad remains based in the United Kingdom and has yet to release the full matchday lineup.

sections.heading: Silent medical report in camp
sections.body: Today’s coverage includes no specific updates regarding the physical condition of the squad following the loss to England. The medical staff has not released information on player availability or new concerns since the 73-8 defeat in London. The focus in the build-up remains on the selection of Virimi Vakatawa and the integration of players from the wider touring group.

sections.heading: Townsend makes wholesale Scottish changes
sections.body: Gregor Townsend has named an almost entirely changed Scotland team, making 14 rotations to the side that lost to South Africa. Only Gregor Brown keeps his place in a selection featuring first starts for hooker Gregor Hiddleston and flanker Freddy Douglas. Leading try scorers Darcy Graham and Duhan van der Merwe return to the wings as the Scots seek to build depth.

sections.heading: Final chance for summer points
sections.body: Fiji goes into the weekend at the bottom of the Nations Championship log after consecutive losses to Wales and England. Having conceded 112 points across two rounds, the squad needs a victory to climb the standings and avoid finishing the opening block in 12th place. The Flying Fijians can take confidence from their 29-14 win over Scotland last year.

sections.heading: Unchanged starting XV for Argentina test
sections.body: Steve Borthwick has named an unchanged starting XV for the trip to Santiago del Estero following the 73-8 demolition of Fiji. Marcus Smith continues at fullback, with Fin Smith preferred at fly-half. The squad has moved hotels in Buenos Aires to avoid noise from local football celebrations after thousands took to the streets following England's 2-1 World Cup semi-final loss to Argentina on Wednesday.

sections.heading: Training intensifies with healthy squad
sections.body: Training has focused on high-intensity drills as the squad prepares for the flight north with no reported fitness concerns. Borthwick described a "really positive week of preparation" following the physical encounter in Johannesburg and the high-scoring win over Fiji. The coaching staff is prioritising recovery and set-piece precision to maintain the momentum gained from last weekend’s nine-try performance in Liverpool.

sections.heading: Argentina targets set-piece improvement
sections.body: Felipe Contepomi’s side enters the weekend on a high after a 35-21 victory over Wales, though the Pumas have not yet officially named their matchday squad. While they are expected to bring characteristic aggression to the breakdown, analysts have identified the Argentinian scrum as a potential Achilles heel. England’s front row will look to target the set-piece early to disrupt the hosts' rhythm.

sections.heading: England enters weekend in seventh place
sections.body: England enters the weekend seventh in the overall log after collecting five points from their opening two fixtures. A victory in Santiago del Estero would see them leapfrog Argentina and potentially move into the top four, depending on results elsewhere. With South Africa and Ireland leading the championship on 10 points each, a bonus-point win is essential to keep pace with the frontrunners.

match.venue: Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades, Santiago del Estero

sections.heading: Galthie rotates front row for Japan test
sections.body: Fabien Galthie has overhauled his pack for Saturday’s Test, naming a completely new front row. The coach stated he was "not completely satisfied" with the scrum performance in recent outings. The reshuffled starting XV features an experienced spine including Romain Ntamack, Maxime Lucu, and Matthieu Jalibert, as France looks to refine its execution following last week’s impressive 42-26 victory over Australia.

sections.heading: Penaud remains sidelined
sections.body: Damian Penaud is confirmed to be out of the selection for the remainder of this month’s Nations Championship fixtures. The winger continues his recovery from a calf injury sustained during the tournament opener. Beyond this long-term absence, the squad’s training focus has shifted toward integrating the rotated starters as the team completes its final preparations for the upcoming encounter in Tokyo.

sections.heading: Japan bolstered by seasoned core
sections.body: Japan has named a side featuring veterans Warner Dearns, Jack Cornelsen, Naoto Saito, and Dylan Riley. After a defeat to Ireland in Round 2, the Brave Blossoms—ranked 11th—will look to improve their discipline. France holds a historical advantage, having won four of the last five meetings between the nations, though Les Bleus anticipate a rigorous challenge from Eddie Jones’s side this weekend.

sections.heading: France eyes climb up tournament log
sections.body: France enters this fixture sitting 4th in the overall standings, holding a positive points difference of 14. With 7 points secured from their opening two matches, a victory in Tokyo is vital to keep pace with the leaders. A strong result on Saturday would provide essential momentum as the team moves into the second half of the 2026 Nations Championship campaign.

sections.heading: Farrell recalls stars for Eden Park challenge
sections.body: Andy Farrell has made nine changes to his starting side for the trip to Auckland, bringing back key personnel who were rested against Japan. Hugo Keenan, Garry Ringrose, Sam Prendergast, Jamison Gibson-Park, Dan Sheehan, Tadhg Furlong, Joe McCarthy, and Josh van der Flier all return. Robert Baloucoune starts on the wing after overcoming a hamstring issue, while Dunedin-born Sean Jansen is named among the replacements.

sections.heading: Full squad available for selection
sections.body: In today's coverage, Andy Farrell reported that Ireland have a healthy squad, confirming they have reached this stage of the summer series with a full complement of 36 players available for selection. The coach expressed his satisfaction with the current state of the touring party ahead of the fixture.

sections.heading: Rennie brings back first-choice spine
sections.body: Dave Rennie has named what appears to be a full-strength All Blacks lineup, making five changes. Josh Moorby starts at fullback, while Tupou Vaa’i moves to blindside flanker. The Hurricanes core of Cam Roigard, Ruben Love, and Jordie Barrett will dictate the 9-10-12 axis. Rennie noted that his selection rewards players who have impressed, emphasizing the importance of impact from the bench to influence the game.

sections.heading: Tournament momentum on the line
sections.body: Both nations enter the match with perfect 2-0 records in the Nations Championship. Ireland leads the Northern Hemisphere standings and victory would represent a significant step toward a spot in the inaugural final. However, they face a formidable historical challenge: the All Blacks remain unbeaten in 52 consecutive tests at this venue, a streak dating back to 1994.

sections.heading: Quesada suspended as Italy ring changes
sections.body: Gonzalo Quesada will miss this weekend’s match after receiving an automatic two-match ban for comments made about referee Luc Ramos following the loss to New Zealand. While the Italian Rugby Federation intends to appeal, the coach is barred from all match-day activity. Italy have responded to their recent defeats by naming a side with nine changes, including a first Test start for forward Giulio Marini.

sections.heading: Rotation and absences reshape the squad
sections.body: The squad is managing several notable absences for this fixture. Outside-backs Tommaso Allan and Malik Faissal remain in doubt after sustaining injuries in the loss to New Zealand. Additionally, star prop Simone Ferrari and Sebastian Negri have not made the trip, while Niccolò Cannone is unavailable due to a four-week suspension following his red card for head-butting in the previous round.

sections.heading: Wallabies gamble on selection for Schmidt’s finale
sections.body: Australia have named Carlo Tizzano at openside flanker for his hometown debut, relegating the impactful Fraser McReight to the bench. In a further selection gamble, Declan Meredith is retained at flyhalf. Joe Schmidt, overseeing his final Test in charge, has also called up Miles Amatosero to the bench for a potential debut. The Wallabies are seeking to break a six-match losing streak on home soil.

sections.heading: Seeking a breakthrough in Perth
sections.body: Italy arrive at this fixture looking for their first points of the Nations Championship after consecutive defeats to Japan and New Zealand. With both teams positioned at the bottom of the log, the match represents a critical opportunity for the visitors to secure momentum and translate their previous success against Australia—including last year’s win in Udine—into a victory on foreign territory.

sections.heading: Townsend makes 14 changes for Murrayfield finale
sections.body: Gregor Townsend has overhauled his side for the final match of the summer series, retaining only Gregor Brown from the starting XV that faced South Africa. Stafford McDowall will captain a team featuring the return of Darcy Graham and Duhan van der Merwe. Townsend noted his focus is on “creating depth” for the future, adding, “there’s some quality players there who should bring lots of energy.”

sections.heading: Macbeth ruled out with leg infection
sections.body: Nathan Macbeth is the only player ruled out of Saturday’s Test, having developed a leg infection in recent days. Otherwise, the squad is prepared for the final rotation. With the coaching staff aiming to ensure everyone on tour receives an opportunity to play, the focus in training has remained on integrating the newer members of the group while maintaining intensity for the final home fixture.

sections.heading: Fiji yet to confirm matchday squad
sections.body: Fiji has not yet announced their lineup for the encounter at Murrayfield. The Flying Fijians are seeking their first tournament win after heavy defeats against Wales and England. Forwards coach John Dalziel has cautioned that Scotland expects a significant backlash from the visitors, noting that the team is ready to address the tactical lessons learned following the recent high-scoring result in Pretoria.

sections.heading: Final push to close out summer campaign
sections.body: Scotland enters this fixture sitting sixth in the overall log. Following a victory in Argentina and a narrow defeat against South Africa, the squad looks to finish the block with a win on home soil. While technically classified as a Fiji ‘home’ game for competition purposes, the match offers the Scots a final opportunity to secure points before the conclusion of this tournament segment.

sections.heading: Tandy freshens side with six changes
sections.body: Steve Tandy has rung the changes for the final Test, introducing six new starters to the side that fell to Argentina. Louis Rees-Zammit returns on the wing, while the midfield is entirely revamped with Ben Thomas and Max Llewellyn pairing up. Dan Edwards earns a start at fly-half, with Teddy Williams and Alex Mann joining the pack to bolster the side against the physical challenge.

sections.heading: Captain Lake clears fitness hurdle
sections.body: Dewi Lake has been cleared to lead the side after overcoming a groin injury sustained in San Juan. The captain came through two training sessions this week to retain his starting role. Elsewhere, Taine Plumtree remains sidelined with a knock, while Ben Carter is unavailable due to injury, resulting in the inclusion of Freddie Thomas on the bench as cover following his arrival in Durban.

sections.heading: Springboks hand debuts to four players
sections.body: Rassie Erasmus has opted for a heavily rotated side, making 10 changes to the team that defeated Scotland. The hosts have handed Test debuts to four players: Carlu Sadie, Ruben van Heerden, Vusi Moyo, and Jaco Williams. Despite the turnover, the Springboks retain a familiar core in captain Malcolm Marx and Pieter-Steph du Toit, looking to maintain their dominant form and unbeaten tournament start.

sections.heading: Final push to climb the log
sections.body: Wales enter this final fixture looking to recover from the setback in Argentina and improve their standing. Currently level on five points with England, a result against the world champions would provide a significant boost to their tournament points tally. The squad is focused on finishing their campaign on a high note by executing their game plan against the physical intensity of the Springboks.

sections.heading: Contepomi makes two changes for England test
sections.body: Head coach Felipe Contepomi has confirmed two adjustments to his squad for Saturday’s meeting with England in Santiago del Estero. The side looks to maintain momentum following their 35-21 victory over Wales in Round 2. Contepomi recently highlighted that rugby union is currently enjoying a significant boom in Argentina, capturing the imagination of a nation otherwise gripped by World Cup fever.

sections.heading: Squad managing physical demands
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage as the squad completes their final preparations. The focus remains on squad conditioning and tactical alignment ahead of the weekend. Players are balancing the intense training load with the logistical challenges of their tournament itinerary, ensuring all members of the group remain available for selection as they look to build on their current standing in the tournament.

sections.heading: Borthwick retains unchanged starting lineup
sections.body: England coach Steve Borthwick has opted for consistency, naming an unchanged starting XV for this clash. The side, led by captain Jamie George, includes experienced internationals such as Ellis Genge and Marcus Smith. Despite some internal distractions regarding their hotel base in Buenos Aires and local World Cup celebrations, the touring party is focused on following up their dominant 73-8 performance against Fiji.

sections.heading: Pumas look to climb tournament log
sections.body: Argentina enters the weekend sitting on 6 points, aiming to improve their current log position with a victory against England. After bouncing back from an opening loss to Scotland by defeating Wales, the team seeks to capitalize on home advantage in Santiago del Estero. A win is essential for the Pumas to consolidate their position in the southern hemisphere standings as the Nations Championship enters its third round.

sections.heading: Schmidt shuffles pack for final farewell
sections.body: Outgoing coach Joe Schmidt has made tactical adjustments for his final Test in charge, handing Carlo Tizzano a hometown start at openside flanker in place of Fraser McReight. Declan Meredith retains the flyhalf jersey despite Ben Donaldson returning to the matchday squad on the bench. Harry Potter replaces the injured Dylan Pietsch on the wing, while Miles Amatosero is set for his Wallabies debut from the reserves.

sections.heading: Pietsch sidelined as Meredith carries on
sections.body: Dylan Pietsch is ruled out of the weekend's Test due to a calf injury sustained in the previous round. Ben Donaldson has proven his fitness following his own recent calf complaint, though he will begin this fixture amongst the replacements. The squad continues their final preparations in Perth, with no further updates regarding fitness concerns reported in the latest coverage following the loss to France.

sections.heading: Italy hit by coach ban and nine changes
sections.body: Italy will be without head coach Gonzalo Quesada, who is serving a two-match suspension for comments made regarding match officials. On the field, the Azzurri have made nine changes to their starting lineup, including a return to fullback for Lorenzo Pani and a start for Giulio Marini. The visitors are looking to rebound after significant defeats to Japan and New Zealand in their opening tournament fixtures.

sections.heading: Momentum the goal in final round fixture
sections.body: Both nations enter this fixture seeking their first points of the 2026 Nations Championship. Following losses to Ireland and France, Australia aims to end a six-match losing streak and avoid a result that would leave the outgoing coaching staff with the lowest win percentage of the professional era. The outcome in Perth is vital for both sides to regain confidence before the November Test window.

sections.heading: Japan reshuffles pack for French test
sections.body: Japan has implemented significant strategic changes for the upcoming clash, revamping the front row and making three further adjustments to the starting XV. Captain Warner Dearns leads the side, with Takuro Matsunaga, Kazuma Ueda, and Dylan Riley all returning to the starting lineup. The coaching staff is banking on these fresh combinations to provide a more potent challenge against the world-class French opposition.

sections.heading: Conditioning focus in Tokyo camp
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today’s coverage regarding the Japan squad. The team has concentrated its training intensity on tactical cohesion and set-piece efficiency throughout the week. Following the physical battle against Ireland last weekend, medical staff continue to monitor the squad’s recovery as they prepare for the high-tempo demands expected against a formidable French outfit at the National Stadium.

sections.heading: France names side featuring Jalibert shift
sections.body: France has confirmed their 23-man squad, with Matthieu Jalibert named at fullback despite missing earlier training sessions. Their lineup showcases a powerful core including Emmanuel Meafou, Romain Ntamack, and Maxime Lucu. Ranked No. 4 globally, the French are tournament favorites and will look to maintain their recent dominance, having won four of the last five encounters between these two nations.

sections.heading: Points vital for tournament progression
sections.body: Sitting at 9 of 12 on the log, Japan looks to bounce back from last week's loss to Ireland. Every point remains critical as the tournament reaches its midpoint. While France occupies a higher position in the current standings, Japan’s goal is to secure a result that validates their recent internal changes and improves their overall standing before the final rounds begin.

sections.heading: Rennie shuffles backline for Ireland test
sections.body: Dave Rennie has named his starting XV for the Nations Championship clash at Eden Park, featuring four changes in personnel. Josh Moorby earns a start on the wing, while Quinn Tupaea returns to the midfield. In the pack, Josh Lord and Patrick Tuipulotu return at lock, with Tupou Vaa’i shifting to blindside flanker. Rennie noted, “We have selected a strong team that allows some of the combinations we have tested to continue building.”

sections.heading: Leroy Carter ruled out of Saturday's Test
sections.body: Leroy Carter has been excluded from the matchday squad for the weekend due to injury, opening the door for Josh Moorby’s elevation to the starting lineup. The wider squad has maintained its training intensity throughout the week as the team looks to refine combinations for the Ireland fixture. No further injury concerns were reported following last week’s victory over Italy.

sections.heading: Farrell leads Ireland into Eden Park fortress
sections.body: Ireland have arrived in Auckland aiming to become the first side to defeat New Zealand at Eden Park in 52 Tests. Coach Andy Farrell has expressed that his squad feels “lucky” to have another opportunity at the venue. Their lineup includes Sam Prendergast at flyhalf and a refreshed pack, as they seek to build on their unbeaten start to the Nations Championship campaign.

sections.heading: Tournament momentum on the line in Auckland
sections.body: New Zealand and Ireland both enter the encounter with perfect two-win records from the opening rounds of the Nations Championship. With both teams positioned at the top of the standings, this match serves as a crucial fixture for the tournament’s progression. A victory for either side would provide a significant advantage in the pursuit of a place in the inaugural Nations Championship Final.

sections.heading: New look Springboks prepare for Wales
sections.body: Rassie Erasmus has rung the changes for the final round, naming an experimental side with 10 alterations from the team that defeated Scotland. Four players will earn their first caps as the Springboks look to maintain their unbeaten run. Vusi Moyo makes his highly anticipated debut at fly-half, joined in the starting lineup by new faces Carlu Sadie, Ruben van Heerden, and Jaco Williams.

sections.heading: Training intensity remains high in Durban
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Springbok squad. The group has been focused on tactical preparation and conditioning at their Durban base following the win against Scotland. The coaching staff has managed player loads carefully throughout the tournament, allowing the team to integrate new starters while ensuring the group maintains the physical edge required for their final Nations Championship fixture.

sections.heading: Captain Lake leads touring Wales
sections.body: Wales head coach Steve Tandy has confirmed captain Dewi Lake will lead the side after overcoming a groin injury scare. Wales has made six changes, opting for a 6-2 split on the bench to counter South Africa’s physical approach. The visitors are looking for a reaction following their recent loss to Argentina, with Louis Rees-Zammit returning to the starting XV to add pace to the backline.

sections.heading: Springboks chase clean sweep in final round
sections.body: South Africa enters the final fixture aiming to preserve their position at the top of the log and consolidate their standing in the world rankings. Having successfully navigated the opening two rounds, the team carries significant momentum into the weekend. A victory in Durban would secure an unbeaten tournament run, capping a successful period of rotation and development under the current coaching cycle.
